title: Taramasalata
subtitle: Greek smoked cod roe dip
excerpt: Taramasalata is a pale pink Greek dip made by emulsifying smoked cod roe with bread, lemon juice, and olive oil. The result is smooth and creamy with a smoky, salty depth. Made properly it is nothing like the bright pink version sold in supermarkets.
sourceNotes: Taramasalata is a staple of Greek mezze. The word tarama refers to the salted and cured roe of grey mullet or cod. The dish is traditionally associated with Lent, when it provided a rich, filling food during fasting periods that excluded meat. In British shops, smoked cod roe paste (sold as tarama) is the practical substitute.

recipe.makeAheadNotes: Keeps in the fridge for up to 3 days.

glossaryTerms.definition: Salted and cured fish roe, traditionally from grey mullet or cod. Sold in British shops as a smoked cod roe paste in jars or sachets. The base ingredient for taramasalata.
To combine two liquids that do not normally mix, such as oil and water, by whisking or blending vigorously so the oil breaks into tiny droplets suspended throughout the mixture.

content.text: Taramasalata is a smooth, creamy dip made by
) with soaked bread, lemon juice, and olive oil. It has a smoky, briny depth and a silky texture. The pale pink colour comes from the roe itself; if yours looks bright pink, the roe is artificial.
The process is like making mayonnaise: add the olive oil slowly and the emulsion stays smooth. Add it too fast and it breaks into oily pools.

items.prepNote: crusts removed, soaked in cold water for 5 minutes then squeezed dry

content.text: Soak the bread slices in cold water for 5 minutes. Squeeze out as much liquid as possible and tear into rough pieces.
Place the tarama, soaked bread, lemon juice, and garlic (if using) in a food processor. Blitz until the mixture is combined and fairly smooth.

With the machine running, pour in {{olive-oil}} of olive oil in a very thin, steady stream, as if making mayonnaise. The dip will become pale and creamy as the oil emulsifies in. Stop and scrape down the sides halfway through.

Taste and add more lemon juice and a pinch of salt if needed. If using, stir in the grated red onion by hand. Chill for at least 30 minutes before serving. Serve with bread for dipping and a drizzle of olive oil.

items.symptom: The dip is oily and separated
items.fix: Spoon off the oily layer. Restart with a fresh piece of squeezed bread in the processor, then slowly blend the separated mixture back in as if it were the oil.

items.cause: Tarama is heavily cured and already salty
items.fix: Add more soaked bread to dilute, or a squeeze more lemon juice to balance. Do not add any extra salt until you have tasted the finished dip.

items.symptom: The texture is grainy rather than smooth
items.cause: Bread not soaked long enough or not squeezed dry
items.fix: Blend for longer. If it's still grainy, pass the dip through a sieve, pressing it through with the back of a spoon.

content.text: Taramasalata appears on Greek mezze tables alongside hummus, olives, and bread. In Greece it is eaten especially during Lent, when fasting rules allow fish products but not meat. Outside Greece it is one of the most widely known Greek foods, though the shop-bought version bears little resemblance to the home-made one. Serve chilled with warm pitta.
